active_paradigms.idea: Implement a deterministic keyword‑rule classifier using compiled regular expressions for each coarse category.
active_paradigms.description: Parse the evaluation dataset once to collect all FM‑ids that belong to C1, C2 and C3. For each category build a list of representative keywords and phrases (e.g., "ignore constraint", "role violation", "repeat work" for C1; "conversation reset", "no clarification", "derailment" for C2; "premature termination", "missing verification" for C3). Compile a single regex per category with `re.compile('|'.join(keywords), re.IGNORECASE)`. In the `classify` function, run each regex on the (trimmed) trace text; if a match is found set the label to True, otherwise False. Return the JSON object exactly as required. This eliminates LLM call latency and variance, giving consistent precision for clearly worded traces while still falling back to False when no cue is present.
active_paradigms.what_to_optimize: Increase macro‑F1 by boosting precision and recall for categories that have distinctive lexical cues; reduce false positives caused by LLM hallucination.
active_paradigms.cautions: Ensure the keyword lists are exhaustive enough to cover synonyms; avoid overly broad patterns that cause many false positives. Keep the regexes simple to stay within prompt size limits (no LLM call needed). Test on a validation split to prune noisy keywords before final deployment.

active_paradigms.idea: Train three independent binary logistic‑regression classifiers on bag‑of‑words features extracted from the traces.
active_paradigms.description: Load `eval_traces.json` and for each trace compute a TF‑IDF vector (using `sklearn.feature_extraction.text.TfidfVectorizer`). Convert the ground‑truth MAST annotations to three binary target columns via `sklearn.preprocessing.MultiLabelBinarizer` based on `CATEGORY_MAP`. Fit three `sklearn.linear_model.LogisticRegression` models (one per C1, C2, C3) with `solver='liblinear'` and `class_weight='balanced'` to handle class imbalance. Serialize the fitted models and the vectorizer as module globals. In `classify`, transform the incoming trajectory with the vectorizer and predict probabilities; threshold at 0.5 (or a tuned value) to produce boolean labels, then output the JSON object. This data‑driven approach can capture patterns the LLM misses and provides deterministic predictions.
active_paradigms.what_to_optimize: Maximize macro‑F1 and label accuracy by learning statistical patterns from the full dataset; improve recall on subtle cases where keyword rules fail.
active_paradigms.cautions: Training must be performed offline; ensure the vectorizer and models are loaded only once to avoid repeated heavy computation. Use a reasonable `max_features` (e.g., 5000) to keep memory low. Validate the probability threshold on a held‑out split to avoid over‑fitting.

tried_paradigms.idea: Enrich the prompt with few‑shot examples and explicit keyword cues for each category, and ask the model to reason step‑by‑step before outputting the JSON.
tried_paradigms.description: Rewrite CLASSIFICATION_PROMPT to start with a short system‑style description, then provide 2–3 miniature trace excerpts that illustrate a positive and a negative case for C1, C2, and C3. After the examples, ask the model to first list the evidence it sees for each category (a one‑sentence bullet list) and finally to return ONLY a JSON object with keys C1, C2, C3. This chain‑of‑thought format guides the LLM to focus on the exact patterns that define the coarse FM categories and reduces hallucination. Keep the trimming logic unchanged; only the prompt text changes. The rest of the code (llm_call, JSON extraction) stays the same.
tried_paradigms.what_to_optimize: Increase macro‑F1 by improving recall and precision for each label through clearer instruction and concrete exemplars.
tried_paradigms.cautions: Make sure the examples are short enough to fit within the token budget after trimming; avoid stray commas or extra braces that could break JSON parsing. Use deterministic temperature (the default model is deterministic), and keep the final JSON on a single line so the regex extractor finds it reliably.

tried_paradigms.idea: Apply a self‑consistency ensemble: call the LLM three times per trace with the same prompt, collect the three JSON outputs, and take the majority vote for each label using statistics.mode.
tried_paradigms.description: Wrap the existing classify function in a new wrapper that repeats llm_call three times (or an odd number) and parses each response into a dict. Then, for each key C1‑C3, feed the three boolean values to statistics.mode to obtain the majority decision. Return the aggregated dict as the final prediction. This reduces random mis‑classifications caused by occasional token‑level noise while keeping the same prompt and trimming logic.
tried_paradigms.what_to_optimize: Boost label accuracy and macro‑F1 by lowering false positives/negatives through majority voting.
tried_paradigms.cautions: The LLM model used (gpt‑oss‑120b) is largely deterministic, but temperature‑like sampling may still produce variation; ensure the wrapper respects any rate limits (use a short sleep between calls if needed). If all three calls return different values (e.g., [True, False, False]), statistics.mode will raise a StatisticsError – handle this by falling back to the first response or using the most common value via collections.Counter.

tried_paradigms.idea: Add few‑shot exemplars to the classification prompt so the LLM sees concrete labeled traces before the target trace.
tried_paradigms.description: Create a small library of 3–4 short example traces (≈200 tokens each) that cover all combinations of C1, C2, C3 (e.g., only C1, C1+C2, C2+C3, all three). For each example include the exact JSON output. Insert these examples at the top of CLASSIFICATION_PROMPT, followed by a clear separator and the target trace. Keep the total prompt under the model’s token limit by trimming the target trace as before. The LLM will then mimic the demonstrated format and learn the boundary cues for each category, leading to more consistent boolean predictions.
tried_paradigms.cautions: Make sure the combined prompt length stays below the model’s context window; use concise examples; verify that the JSON extraction regex still works with the added examples; avoid duplicate category wording that could confuse the model.

tried_paradigms.idea: Introduce a rule‑based keyword/regex fallback that scans the trace for category‑specific indicator phrases and sets the booleans before optionally consulting the LLM for verification.
tried_paradigms.description: Define three keyword lists (one per category) containing high‑precision phrases such as "ignores constraints" or "role violation" for C1, "conversation reset" or "no clarification" for C2, and "premature termination" or "missing verification" for C3. Compile each list into a case‑insensitive regular expression using re.compile and run them on the (trimmed) trace. The presence of any match sets the corresponding label to True. If no keyword is found for a category, optionally call the LLM with a shortened prompt that includes the already‑detected labels as context, letting the model confirm or correct them. Return the final boolean map.
tried_paradigms.what_to_optimize: reduce false positives/negatives, improve label accuracy and overall macro‑F1
tried_paradigms.cautions: Curate the keyword lists carefully to avoid over‑general matches; limit regex scans to the trimmed trace to keep runtime low; ensure the fallback LLM call does not exceed token limits; handle cases where multiple keywords appear for the same category gracefully.
